5.caption: This page does not contain any rules diagrams, flowcharts, or illustrated walkthroughs. It features a visual key explaining a formatting convention for the rulebook, showing a green-highlighted box with the text "Example App Reference" to demonstrate how links to the Warhammer 40,000 app will be displayed. The page also includes a large QR code for players to scan to download the companion app, displayed below an image of the app's icon. A photograph at the bottom of the page shows several Ork miniatures on a desert-themed battlefield with terrain, but this is a general illustration and not a specific rules example.

11.caption:
This page features a detailed breakdown of a Warhammer 40,000 datasheet for an Ork BOYZ unit, using seven numbered callouts to explain its different sections.

Callout 1 identifies the unit's name, BOYZ.

Callout 2 points to the characteristics profiles for the two model types in the unit, the BOYZ and the BOSS NOB. Both have a Movement of 6 inches, Toughness 5, a 5+ Save, Leadership 7+, and an Objective Control of 2. The standard Boy has 1 Wound, while the Boss Nob has 2 Wounds.

Callout 3 highlights the unit's Abilities. These include the Faction ability 'Waaagh!' and a special rule called 'Get da Good Bitz', which allows the unit to secure an objective it controls at the end of the Command phase.

Callout 4 indicates the weapon profile tables for both ranged and melee weapons. This section lists the statistics for weapons like the kustom shoota, kombi-rokkit, shoota, slugga, big choppa, and choppa, detailing their Range, Attacks, Skill, Strength, Armour Penetration, and Damage.

Callout 5 shows the Keywords section at the bottom of the datasheet. The unit has the keywords INFANTRY, BATTLELINE, MOB, EXPLOSIVES, and BOYZ. The Faction Keyword for the unit is ORKS.

Callout 6 points to the Unit Composition section, which states that the unit is composed of 1 Boss Nob and 9 Boyz. It also lists their default wargear.

Callout 7 identifies the Wargear Options section, which details how a player can customize the unit's equipment. For this unit, the Boss Nob can replace his kustom shoota with one kombi-shoota and one kombi-rokkit.

18.caption:
This page details the first two steps of the four-step Attack Sequence: Hit Rolls and Wound Rolls.

A flowchart for Hit Rolls shows how to determine the outcome of rolling a D6. An unmodified roll of a die showing one pip is a Fail, highlighted in red. An unmodified roll of a die showing six pips is a Critical Hit, highlighted in green. A roll that is equal to or greater than the attacking model's Ballistic Skill (BS) or Weapon Skill (WS) characteristic is a Hit, also in green. Any other result is a Fail.

A second flowchart explains Wound Rolls. An unmodified roll of a one is a Fail. An unmodified roll of a six is a Critical Wound. A roll that is equal to or greater than a required result is a Wound. All fails are shown in red, while successes are green.

A table titled 'Attack's Strength vs Target's Toughness' determines the required result for a Wound. If the attacker's Strength is twice or more than the target's Toughness, the required roll is 2+. If Strength is greater than Toughness, it's 3+. If Strength is equal to Toughness, it's 4+. If Strength is less than Toughness, it's 5+. If Strength is half or less than half the Toughness, the required roll is 6+.

20.caption:
This page features a diagram illustrating the "Making Attacks" part of the attack sequence.

In this example, a RED unit of three models is attacking a BLUE unit of five models. The attacking player selects all of the RED unit's weapons: two boltguns, labeled B; two bolt pistols, labeled BP; and one heavy bolter, labeled HB. The BLUE unit is selected as the target. The diagram shows that all attacking models have a line of sight to the target unit, but one of the bolt pistols is out of range and cannot make an attack, which is indicated by a red X over its icon.

To resolve the attacks, the player gathers attack dice. Weapons that make identical attacks are grouped together. The two boltguns and the one in-range bolt pistol are grouped into a single pool of five attack dice. The accompanying text explains that boltguns have an Attacks characteristic of 2 and bolt pistols have an Attacks characteristic of 1. The heavy bolter does not make an identical attack, so it is resolved separately. With an Attacks characteristic of 3, it generates its own pool of three attack dice. The diagram shows these two distinct dice pools, one with five dice and one with three.

21.caption:
This page provides two illustrated examples of the attack sequence, showing how dice rolls are resolved.

In the first example, titled 'Resolving Attack Dice,' a unit makes five attacks with its boltguns and bolt pistol, which are labeled B and BP. The walkthrough follows four steps. First, for Hit Rolls, the attacker needs a 3+ based on their Ballistic Skill (BS), and four of the five attacks hit the target. Second, for Wound Rolls, the weapon's Strength (S) of 4 against the target's Toughness (T) of 3 requires a 3+ to wound, and three attacks are successful. Third, the defending player makes three Save Rolls. Finally, in the Inflict Damage step, the results are determined based on the target's Save (Sv) of 3+ and Invulnerable Save (InSv) of 5+. One save roll fails, which destroys a model, while the other two saves are passed.

The second example, 'Resolving Other Attacks,' shows a unit making three attacks with a heavy bolter (HB). For the Hit Rolls, the weapon's BS of 4+ results in two successful hits. For the Wound Rolls, the weapon's S of 5 against the target's T of 3 results in both hits wounding. For the Save Rolls, the heavy bolter's Armour Penetration (AP) characteristic of -1 applies a penalty to the defender's dice rolls. One save roll fails after this modification, destroying a model, while the other save is successful.

22.caption:
This page provides an example of the attack sequence, specifically for attacking an Attached Unit.

A battlefield diagram shows a RED unit attacking a BLUE unit. The RED unit is represented by tokens indicating its weapons: seven models with boltguns (B), two with heavy bolters (HB), and one with a plasma pistol (PP). The BLUE unit is an Attached Unit, composed of a Seraphim unit joined by Saint Celestine and her two Geminae Superia.

The example follows three steps of the attack sequence. First, the RED unit selects all its weapons to attack. Second, the BLUE Attached Unit is selected as the target. Third, the attacking player gathers attack dice to resolve the attacks. The player decides to resolve the attacks from different weapon types separately, starting with the heavy bolters.

The diagram illustrates how the number of attack dice is calculated. The two heavy bolters, each with an Attacks characteristic of 3, generate six attack dice. The seven boltguns, each with an Attacks characteristic of 2, generate fourteen attack dice. The single plasma pistol, with an Attacks characteristic of 1, generates one attack die.

23.caption:
This page provides a visual walkthrough of the "Allocation Groups" rule. The diagram shows an attack from two Heavy Bolters that has scored five wounds against a single unit. The target unit's controlling player divides it into three allocation groups and declares the order in which they will take saves. The first group consists of two Geminae Superia models, labeled with a '1'. The second group contains several Seraphim models, labeled with a '2'. The final group is the CHARACTER model Saint Celestine, labeled with a '3', who must be chosen last.

The defending player makes five save rolls, resulting in two 1s, a 3, a 4, and a 5. Attacks are resolved one at a time, from the lowest save roll to the highest. The two rolls of 1 are allocated to the first group, the Geminae Superia. Both saves fail, and both Geminae Superia models are destroyed. The next lowest roll, a 3, is allocated to the next group in the order, the Seraphim. The text explains that the attacking weapon's AP of -1 causes this save to fail, destroying one Seraphim model. The remaining two save rolls are successful, so no further damage is inflicted.

25.caption:
This page features four diagrams illustrating visibility rules.

The first diagram, for the "Model Visible" rule, shows a single blue Space Marine model partially hidden behind a large industrial pipe. Only the model's head, one shoulder, and backpack are visible to the observer, demonstrating that if any part of a model can be seen, that model is considered visible.

The second diagram, for "Model Fully Visible," shows a single blue Space Marine model standing in the open with no terrain blocking the view. This illustrates that a model is fully visible when every part of it facing the observer can be seen, with no intervening objects blocking line of sight.

The third diagram, for "Unit Visible," shows a unit of five blue Space Marine models near ruined walls. Some of the models are partially obscured by the terrain, but at least one model is clearly visible. This demonstrates that a unit is considered visible if one or more models in that unit are visible to the observer.

The final diagram, for "Unit Fully Visible," shows a unit of five blue Space Marine models in an open area. The rule states that a unit is fully visible if every model in it is fully visible. The diagram and text clarify that for the purpose of determining this, the observing model can see through other models in the target unit, meaning models do not block line of sight to other models in their own unit.

29.caption:
This page features a diagram that lists the seven steps of a game turn in sequential order. Each step is numbered and includes a title, a brief description, and a representative icon.

The sequence is as follows:
1. Start of Turn Step: Rules triggered at the start of a turn are resolved. The icon is an hourglass.
2. Command Phase: You marshal strategic resources and check your units' morale. The icon is a spiky sun.
3. Movement Phase: Your units move across the battlefield and strategic reserves arrive. The icon is an upward-pointing arrow.
4. Shooting Phase: Your units make attacks with their ranged weapons. The icon is a targeting reticle.
5. Charge Phase: Your units make charge moves to engage the enemy. The icon is two chevrons.
6. Fight Phase: Both players' units make attacks with their melee weapons. The icon is two crossed hammers.
7. End of Turn Step: Rules triggered at the end of a turn are resolved in a specific order. First, resolve any non-mission rules. Second, both players consult their mission and resolve any achieved aspects. The icon for this step is an hourglass.

31.caption:
This page provides four illustrated examples of the Battle-shock rule.

The first example shows a unit of three Tyranid models with a starting strength of 3. It is not at or below half-strength, but it is currently battle-shocked. Therefore, it must make a battle-shock roll to attempt to recover from this status.

The second example shows a unit of five Space Marine models from a unit with a starting strength of 10. Because it has exactly half of its starting models, it is at half-strength and must make a battle-shock roll.

The third example features a unit of two Space Marine models from a unit with a starting strength of 5. Since the number of remaining models is less than half its starting strength, the unit is below half-strength and must make a battle-shock roll.

The final example shows a Vehicle with a starting strength of 1 and a Wounds (W) characteristic of 11. It has 3 wounds remaining. As this is less than half of its starting Wounds, the Vehicle is considered below half-strength and must make a battle-shock roll.

A "See Also" section directs players to related rules for Battle-shock, including Starting Strength and Half-strength.

33.caption:
This page visually organizes four types of moves a unit can make, each presented in a distinct box with a red and white chevron icon.

The "Remain Stationary" box states that any unit may choose not to move, which does not trigger rules associated with starting or ending a move.

The "Normal Move" box allows an unengaged unit to move a distance up to its M characteristic, and it must end the move unengaged.

The "Advance Move" box allows an unengaged unit to move its M characteristic plus the result of a D6 roll, but the unit cannot declare a charge or start an action for the rest of the turn.

The "Fall-Back Move" box is for an engaged unit. It has two modes: "Ordered Retreat" can be chosen if the unit is not battle-shocked. Otherwise, the unit must make a "Desperate Escape," which requires a hazard roll for each model but allows them to move through enemy models. After any Fall-Back move, the unit must be unengaged and cannot shoot, charge, or start an action that turn. If a unit that was not battle-shocked makes a Desperate Escape, it must then make a battle-shock roll.

35.caption:
This page features three diagrams, each a stylized icon representing a specific type of shooting action.

Next to the Assault Shooting rule, a diagram shows a white, four-pointed star with arrows pointing outwards from its center, symbolizing a unit shooting after making an advance move.

Adjacent to the Close-Quarters Shooting rule, a similar star symbol has arrows pointing inwards toward its center, illustrating a unit that is engaged and firing at the enemy it is in contact with.

Finally, the Indirect Shooting rule is accompanied by a diagram of a star symbol where four dashed, arcing lines travel from the center and land outside the points of the star, visually representing how Indirect Fire weapons can shoot at targets that are not visible by firing over obstacles.

37.caption:
This page features a large diagram titled "MAKING A CHARGE MOVE" that provides a visual example of the charge rules. In the scenario, a BLUE unit declares a charge and its charge roll results in a 7, setting a maximum charge distance of 7 inches. The diagram shows three potential RED enemy units, labeled A, B, and C, to illustrate valid and invalid charge targets.

Unit A is within the 7-inch charge distance, so it can be selected as a charge target. However, the example states that the BLUE unit would not be able to end its charge move engaged with Unit A because of the intervening terrain, making the charge impossible to complete against this target.

Unit B is more than 7 inches away from the charging BLUE unit. Since it is outside the maximum distance generated by the charge roll, it cannot be selected as a charge target.

Group C consists of two separate RED units that are both within the 7-inch charge distance. The example explains that both can be selected as charge targets. Because the charging BLUE unit can successfully end its move engaged with both of its charge targets in group C, this charge move can be made.

38.caption:
This page contains a list and two rules boxes detailing the Fight Phase.

A black box lists the five steps of the Fight phase in order: Start of Fight Phase, Pile In, Fight, Consolidate, and End of Fight Phase.

A small callout box clarifies that while you must fight with all units that can, you are not required to make a pile-in or consolidate move with a unit if you do not want to.

A large rules diagram explains the Pile-in Move. The maximum distance for this move is 3 inches. A unit is eligible to make a pile-in move if it is engaged, it made a charge move this turn, or it was selected for an overrun fight. Before moving, if your unit is already engaged, you must select every enemy unit it is engaged with as pile-in targets; otherwise, you select one or more enemy units within 5 inches of your unit. While moving, models in base-contact with an enemy cannot be moved, and each model that does move must end its move closer to the closest pile-in target and engaged with it if possible. After moving, your unit must be engaged, and any model that started the move engaged with an enemy unit must still be engaged with that same unit.

42.caption:
This page features a large rules diagram for the Consolidation Move, which occurs in step 4 of the Fight Phase. The diagram is a dark grey box that outlines the process for making a 3-inch Consolidation Move. It begins by stating a unit is eligible if it was eligible to fight in the current Fight phase.

The diagram then presents a flowchart under the "BEFORE MOVING" heading, where a player must select one of three consolidation modes. An arrow points to "Ongoing Consolidation" if the unit is already engaged. A second arrow points to "Engaging Consolidation" if the unit is not engaged but is within 3 inches of an enemy unit. A third arrow points to "Objective Consolidation" if the unit is not near enemies but is within 3 inches of an objective.

Following this choice, the "WHILE MOVING" and "AFTER MOVING" sections provide bulleted rules for each of the three modes, detailing how models must move closer to or end engaged with enemies, or end within range of an objective.

A separate callout box titled "NEW FOES TO FACE" provides tactical advice, warning that using the "engaging consolidation" mode to contact enemy units that have not yet fought will give those enemy units a chance to fight your unit.

At the bottom of the page, a red icon shows the number 12 and two crossed hammers, indicating the Fight Phase is the 12th section of the rules.

55.caption:
This page features three diagrams explaining how to read and use Stratagems.

The primary diagram is an example of a Stratagem card for the Core Stratagem "Heroic Intervention". It costs a base of 1 Command Point. The card explains it is used at the end of your opponent's Charge phase to allow a friendly unengaged unit to make a charge move. The player must choose one of two modes. The first mode, "Leap to Defend," has no extra cost and lets the unit charge enemy units that made a charge move this phase. The second mode, "Into the Fray," costs an additional 1 Command Point, indicated by a "+1CP" in a red box. This mode modifies the charge roll and allows the unit to charge any enemy unit within 6 inches. An adjacent text box explains that this is an example of a Stratagem with optional conditions that carry an additional CP cost.

A "SEE ALSO" box provides navigational links to other relevant rules sections. It directs the reader to the "USING STRATAGEMS" section, with specific subsections for "Modifying CP Cost" and "Rules Sequencing".

At the bottom of the page, a "STRATAGEMS KEY" diagram explains the meaning of icons found on Stratagem cards. A skull inside a diamond shape, associated with a green bar, indicates the Stratagem can be used in either player's turn. A skull inside an upward-pointing teardrop, associated with a blue bar, means it is used in your turn. A skull inside a downward-pointing teardrop, associated with a red bar, means it is used in your opponent's turn.

56.caption: On this page, icons are used to visually represent five different Core Stratagems. The Command Re-roll stratagem is marked by a large circular arrow, symbolizing the act of re-rolling dice. The Epic Challenge stratagem, used in the Fight phase, features an icon of two crossed swords to represent a duel. For the Insane Bravery stratagem, which affects a battle-shock roll, the icon is a shining starburst. The Explosives stratagem, used in the Shooting phase, is identified by a target-like symbol with crosshairs. Finally, the Crushing Impact stratagem, used after a charge move, has an icon of two upward-pointing chevrons, suggesting a forceful impact. Each of these stratagems also has a smaller, secondary icon of a skull. The bottom right of the page contains an illustration of a heavily augmented skull, with various tubes and wires connecting it to a large, cylindrical piece of machinery on its back.

57.caption:
This page displays six Core Stratagems and rules, each accompanied by icons that visually summarize their cost, timing, and associated game phase.

The Rapid Ingress Stratagem costs 1 Command Point. Its icons show an upward-pointing arrow, indicating a movement or deployment action, and a skull within a target reticle, signifying that this Stratagem is used during an opponent's turn.

The Fire Overwatch Stratagem costs 1 Command Point. It uses the same icons as Rapid Ingress: an upward-pointing arrow, representing the opponent's Movement phase trigger, and the skull-and-reticle icon for use during an opponent's turn.

The Snap Shooting rule, which is not a Stratagem, is marked by a distinct icon: a red square containing a white, four-pointed star resembling a crosshair, visually flagging it as a special type of shooting attack.

The Smokescreen Stratagem costs 1 Command Point. Its icons are a segmented circle, representing the Shooting phase or effects on visibility, and the skull-and-reticle icon, indicating it is used during an opponent's turn.

The Heroic Intervention Stratagem costs 1 Command Point. Its icons show an upward-pointing chevron, representing a charge, and the skull-and-reticle icon for use during an opponent's turn. A prominent callout box for the "Into the Fray" option within this Stratagem displays a cost of +1CP.

The Counteroffensive Stratagem costs 2 Command Points. Its icons feature two crossed hammers, representing the Fight phase, and the skull-and-reticle icon, indicating it is used during an opponent's turn.

65.caption:
This page contains a flowchart for the Disembark Move rule, detailing how to select one of three disembark modes. The choice depends on the actions of the unit's TRANSPORT model during the current phase. An arrow indicates that if the TRANSPORT made a normal or ingress move, you must choose Rapid Disembark. A second arrow shows that if the TRANSPORT remained stationary or has not yet moved, you must choose Tactical Disembark. A final arrow indicates that in any other case, you must choose Combat Disembark, which requires a hazard roll for each model.

A sidebar provides a specific example for the Rapid Disembark rule. It clarifies that when a unit uses the rapid disembark mode after its TRANSPORT makes an ingress move, the disembarking models must follow the same rules and restrictions as the TRANSPORT. For instance, if the TRANSPORT had to be set up more than 8 inches from all enemy units and not within the opponent's deployment zone, the same restrictions apply to the disembarking unit.

69.caption: A diagram within the Ingress Move rules box illustrates how a unit arrives from Strategic Reserves. It shows a red, chevron-patterned area along a battlefield edge, representing the 6-inch set-up distance specified in the rule. A white arrow points from off the board into this red area, indicating the direction of the ingress move. A circular icon within the zone represents the arriving unit, visually confirming that it must be set up wholly within 6 inches of the battlefield edge.

70.caption:
This page contains a diagram titled "Making a Surge Move" which illustrates the rules for this type of movement.

The diagram shows three units on a battlefield grid: Unit A, Unit B, and Unit C. The example begins after Unit A has shot and destroyed two models from Unit B; the destroyed models are marked with skull icons. According to the text, Unit B has an ability that allows it to make a surge move of D6 inches when its models are destroyed by an enemy's shooting attack. The player rolls a D6, resulting in a 4.

Following the surge move rules, Unit B must move towards the closest enemy unit, which is Unit C. The surviving models of Unit B, which are outlined in red, then move up to 4 inches towards Unit C. An arrow labeled "4"" shows the path of one of Unit B's models. The diagram shows the final position of Unit B's models in base-to-base contact with the models of Unit C, which are outlined in blue. The text confirms that Unit B ends its move engaged with Unit C.

84.caption:
This page features two distinct rule callouts.

The first is a text-based example for the [RAPID FIRE] ability. It explains that if a weapon with the [RAPID FIRE 1] ability and an Attacks (A) characteristic of 1 targets a unit within half of its range, you would gather one additional attack die, for a total of two attack dice for that weapon.

The second is a diagram for the SCOUT MOVE, presented in a dark grey box next to a red and grey chevron graphic. The box details the rules for this move. The MAXIMUM DISTANCE is the X" value from the unit's Scouts X" ability. A unit is ELIGIBLE to make a scout move during the Resolve Pre-battle Abilities step, provided it is wholly within its deployment zone. The EFFECT is that the unit moves following the standard Moving rules (03). The AFTER MOVING condition states that the unit must end its move more than 8 inches horizontally from all enemy units.

87.caption: This page does not contain any rules diagrams, flowcharts, example battlefield layouts, datasheet callouts, or illustrated walkthroughs.
